content:
hi, folks. Currently, i am working on rewrite tty0tty module in rust. i find there are many struct defined in the original C version tty0tty.c 's header file(tty.h tty_driver.h....). i add those header file in the bindgen_helper.rs and i find the follow struct in the binding_generated.rs. But now i don't know how to use it, is there anyone know how to use the struct generated by bindgen? or may i must implement safe abstraction in the rust/kernel crate? but it's hard for me, can i just use it without safe abstraction?
1719220020274.png
Do you understand the C version? You can use it directly (unsafely) if you're just experimenting, but the goal is usually to write safe abstractions and eventually get those in tree
Writing abstractions is hard to do right. But they should be reasonably easy to use correct, and ideally impossible to use incorrectly

You could probably start your project just using the unsafe functions and bindings::tty_driver directly. That's totally fine if you're just figuring out how things work.

If you wanted to get something that others can use though, you'll eventually need to create a struct TtyDriver(Opaque<bindings::tty_driver>) (or a wrapper around pointer, depending on whether that struct is "owned" or not), and then write safe versions of the C functions in the impl TtyDriver block

ok, thanks. Now i can use those struct and function. However, i notice there is other rust version driver module they need to register the driver first. They write a lot of abstraction to wrap the register which is too complex for me. can i direct use the register function in the C side to register them in Rust?
Is your goal just to learn? If so, you can of course do whatever you like

ok, thanks. i understand. I met a new problem, i found the method "with_capacity" in the /rust/alloc/vec/mod.rs, however, when i try to use it in my rust module, it shows "function or associated item not found in Vec<_, _>", is there anyone know how to solve the problem?
image.png
1719319210371.png

That function has #[cfg(not(no_global_oom_handling))], which means that it is disabled in the kernel. You have to import the VecExt trait from the kernel crate instead which provides a with_capacity method that returns a Result: https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/55027e689933ba2e64f3d245fb1ff185b3e7fc81/rust/kernel/alloc/vec_ext.rs#L20

ok, i use another method called "try_with_capacity" and it works. However, there is a problem when i try to register the driver just like in "C" with "let retval = unsafe { bindings::tty_register_driver(tty0tty_tty_driver) };" my VM kernel will panic just like below, do you know why and how to register the driver in Rust(without safe abstraction).
256b3b870765df7e5652461653f2181.png
1ef379b079bb41841ca3018b1101218.png
wenzhao has marked this topic as resolved.
